Transcrição Completa: 27 SDR Setter Best Practices 6 14 25
[00:00] "All right. All right. Welcome into the weekly group call. You guys know what it is. This week we got a request from Brooke. Brooke, you want to articulate yourself for the people or would you like me to articulate it for you?"
[00:12] "Yeah, I can give a brief overview. So, essentially, um, what's happening is we have like we've always had pretty liberal qualifications on our sales calls just to to minimize the amount of cash being left. So, a lot a lot of what's happening now is our STRs. We're canceling a ton of calls. like 30 30% of the calls we booked were being cancelled."
[00:35] "Um, and essentially now we've been putting a lot of work in to hammer them. So to to improve our pre-call process, but what I was worried about happening is like we're very quick. So like someone books a call or SCR's call them within a minute or two."
[00:50] "So essentially someone books a call, STR calls them, but then before hammer them even has a chance to take effect, we're already canceling the call. So I'm just like and essentially they're canceling them for weird reasons that seem to me like um should be handled on the sales call."
[01:10] "like lead, they booked in the call, but then lead um lead's worried about it being a bad time or lead is worried about lead wants to follow up on this in a month or something like that. Like to me, that should seem to be all be addressed on the sales call."
[01:25] "So, um have now tightened the tightened the qualifications. So, we're getting way way more qualified calls. Um not cancelelling them, but now our cost per call is like nearly double. So just trying to find a healthy balance between like allowing the right amount through um not not not letting [__] on the calendar but still um yeah essentially just having a healthy balance between the two."
[01:50] "Sounds fair to me. So that rolls into our topic for today which is going to be SDR best practices. Okay. We're going to make sure that we cover in depth what a setter's job is. Okay. Setters can really make or break revenue if you have them involved. Period."
[02:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Perceived Job of a Setter"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Conceito de qualificação e agendamento, função básica do setter
[02:10] "Just like what Brooke was just saying, they are responsible for qualifying in most instances. Okay, so generally the perceived job of a setter. Okay, the perceived job is for them to qualify and schedule."
[02:25] "Okay. In some instances, setters are intended to qualify and increase the probability of a scheduled call showing up. So, they'll kind of reach out prematurely. To Brook's point, they like when they reach out matters a lot."
[02:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Call Funnel Structure"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Fluxo do funil: Ad → VSSL Page → Application → Call Booking → Confirmation Page → Email Sequences → Setters/Closers Outreach → Hammer Them Campaign
[02:42] "Okay. We talked about this on last week's call where I talked about just some of the pre-call best practices that the sales team should be implementing. And I talked about this concept of um creating prioritization in the lead's mind."
[03:00] "And we generally accomplish that through tailored communication. Okay? Tailored communication can be all kinds of things. We can send somebody sales assets and trust assets that are specific to things that the lead is actually going to give a [__] about while we do the same thing of attempting to qualify."
[03:20] "Okay, this is really important to understand this. When a setter reaches out prematurely, I was actually just talking about this in a YouTube video I was filming on this jet where I said usually as an example in a call funnel what happens is you have the ad as the first step."
[03:38] "You know, obviously they go through the process of the VSSL page applying and booking a call. From there with this back-end selling system that I encourage everybody to implement. We have our confirmation page best practices. We have our value dense email sequences and then simultaneously we have our setters and our closers doing manual outreach and our hammer them campaign."
[04:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "First Interaction Importance"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Representação da primeira interação do setter como parte crítica da experiência do cliente
[04:02] "Okay. What's important to understand is this specific action can generally happen give or take somewhere in between the confirmation page and the value dense email sequences. This step of the setters generally reaches people in one of the first things."
[04:20] "Okay? It's like one of the first interactions that they're going to have as a representation of what your business is like."
[04:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Restaurant Reservation Analogy"
NÚMEROS: Michelin star restaurants mencionados
VISUAL: Comparação entre experiência de reserva em restaurante de luxo vs. atendimento ruim, impacto da primeira impressão
[04:30] "U I'll give you guys a quick, you know, like pulled back example of how important this interaction is. If you go online and you book a reservation at a fancy restaurant, all right, something that you genuinely look forward to going and eating at, maybe it's a Michelin restaurant or just whatever it is."
[04:48] "And most of us, if not all of us, are literally just going to book through some software like Open Table, resi Top Tip, whatever the [__] it is. Uh, moral of the story is what then generally happens is one of the hostess or the reservation management team is going to reach out either the day of or sometimes days before and just confirm that you're actually going to show up."
[05:10] "Has anybody here put in the chat ever had a bad experience with one of those people that reach out to confirm a reservation? It could be as simple as they just sound like a [__] It could they could be that they sound pretentious and you therefore interpreted it as a shitty experience."
[05:28] "Whereas every time you've ever booked at a fancy restaurant somewhere and those reservation people reach out to you and they confirm your reservation, they've done a great job and they've left a very positive impression with you in comparison."
[05:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Internet Marketing vs. Michelin Restaurants"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Comparação entre abordagem profissional de restaurantes Michelin vs. abordagem egoísta de setters em marketing digital
[05:45] "Okay, that initial interaction, right, is very overlooked by most people in the internet marketing space. What I've noticed is is like a fancy restaurant that has a Michelin star, they know what I just said is true. Not only do they know it, they build the experience around that principle that this interaction lit even just down to the reservation booking experience is a part of the experience you have with interacting with their business."
[06:10] "And so they want it to be a great experience. From what I've seen specifically in like this internet money space is setters are perceived for very selfish intentions which are I want to get shitty leads off my calendar to free up my closers and I only want them to reach out to people to qualify."
[06:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Selfish Intent Problem"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Intenção egoísta dos setters transparecendo para os leads, experiência negativa inicial
[06:30] "And when you have that intent it shines through clear as day to the lead and it starts the experience off in a violent way."
[06:38] "So, as an example, like Brooke, when someone reaches out on behalf of your business and they're and and the setter's having that conversation right away after a lead comes in, what's the conversation usually look like?"
[06:50] "Usually ask like why they want to start the business?"
[06:53] "Great question."
[06:54] "Reason why. And then, um, usually trying to assess what sort of budget they have set aside to do so."
[07:02] "So they essentially reach out and they're like, 'Hey, Brooke, what's going on? Jeremy over here with Brooks team wanted to touch base, ask you a few questions. You got two minutes for me. Why Why is it you wanted to start this business?' So, and then you give me some answer and then I follow up with if you don't mind me asking, what budget do you have to actually commit to this thing to make it work? That's the question."
[07:25] "Maybe not exactly that, but we more try and ask indirectly."
[07:28] "How does that sound? something along the lines of um have you started an online business before? Have you joined a program before? What was your experience like? Okay. Um you want to start a business? What types of businesses do you want to start? Do you have something set aside to do so?"
[07:45] "Record these calls."
[07:47] "Yeah, we do."
[07:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "AI Call Analysis Strategy"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Uso de Gong, ChatGPT, Claude para análise de transcrições, automação com N8N
[07:50] "Do you run them through an AI to assess the questions that are asked and like how often they're asked?"
[07:55] "Working on that right now with Gong, but having tech issues."
[07:58] "Understood. I mean, you could do something as simple as just export the transcripts, each one of these calls, import them into chat GBT or claude in the meantime to do a general assessment."
[08:10] "And it's really easy to do once you get them in there. You can go back to that same thread and just upload the calls for that day. So, it's a repetitive task for it. I'm sure you can automate it too with something like NADN."
[08:22] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "LLM Training for Call Assessment"
NÚMEROS: Escala 0-100 mencionada
VISUAL: Treinamento de LLMs para avaliar qualidade das conversas dos setters, escala de avaliação
[08:25] "Moral of the story is you essentially want to know like you want to train one of these LLMs to be able to assess for you like a sales manager would whether these conversations on a scale of like zero to 100 are coming off wildly selfish and one-sided for your organization or whether these questions are as you intend them to be asked in more of like a I don't know just less direct more friendly way I guess we could put it right"
[08:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Data Collection Purpose"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Acumulação de dados para CRM, comunicação personalizada, intenção real da qualificação
[08:52] "the the other and this is really important to understand like when business setter is getting those questions, right? In most instances, businesses are simply asking these questions to get to the point where they can ask the qualification questions that relate to finances."
[09:08] "Okay? When the setters ask these questions, what they're doing is is they're accumulating data that should be inputed into a CRM. That way, the closer who the lead is being handed off to and the setter who's getting that information can then tailor communication that is going to help aid that lead closing in the sales process."
[09:28] "Okay? That is like the ultimate intention of a setter. Okay? Absolutely. If they talk to a [__] dud lead, cancel them off the calendar. Remove them off the calendar."
[09:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Interest Scale"
NÚMEROS: 5 níveis de interesse
VISUAL: Escala vertical de interesse: Curiosity → General Interest → High Interest → Conviction → SOLD (Money Zone entre Conviction e Sold)
[09:40] "But what we always have to remember is is the interest scale. Okay. So, if this represents interest and this just simply represents the lead. All right. We have essentially a few different levels of interest we've got to look at."
[09:55] "First of all, we have curiosity. Then we have general interest. Then we have high interest. Then we have conviction. And generally between conviction and sold, this is like the money zone."
[10:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Money Zone"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Zona destacada entre Conviction e Sold onde negócios são fechados
[10:12] "At this range, this is where deals get done. Okay? Anything below this, there is no money collected. Like you can get people close to this, but if you don't actually get them through that level, then you're not going to get a lead sold."
[10:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "In-Market Buyers Definition"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Características de compradores no mercado: lidando ativamente com problema, priorizaram solução
[10:30] "Now, generally, leads come through with a few different levels of interest. They can come through, as an example, curious or general interest. The only times you really get people who have high interest are people who are actively dealing with the problem and have prioritized solving the problem. Okay, these are inmarket buyers."
[10:50] "So, as an example for Brook's business, Brooke, tell me if I'm way off here. Somebody who's already sold on the idea. They want to start an ecom store. They want to leverage AI to be able to help them do it. U they maybe have already [__] up a few times with ecom and they know that they have a shitty teacher and they look up to you already."
[11:12] "This person's like, 'I love Brooke. I love what he can teach and help me do. I already have these problems and I'm ready to give him money to solve it.' But they might have a handful of questions that need answered in order to still get them to the point where they're at high interest to getting into the conviction or sold category. How how rare does that happen?"
[11:30] "Yeah, that's I would say most people."
[11:33] "So, think about this, right? It's like if that's what most people are coming in at, that's what you're saying a majority of your leads are at."
[11:40] "Most people are like have some questions. They know who I am. They've watched pre-call content, but they have objections either around trust, around how the business model works. Like they have a high like we have like a 20 25 minute force VSSL. So they they have a decent idea of who I am and how everything works. So it's not like they're they have no clue."
[12:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "High vs General Interest Distinction"
NÚMEROS: 20-25 minutos de VSSL
VISUAL: Diferenciação clara entre interesse geral (perguntas) vs. interesse alto (pronto para comprar)
[12:08] "Now, there's a big difference between high and general. What you described there sounds more general to me in comparison to high. High would be like, I'm already ready to give you money. Like, I'm like fully willing to give you money. However, if these few questions don't get answered, like I'm not going to give you the money. If they do get answered, then again, all I got to do is kick up one interest level and I'm there."
[12:32] "If you see that, like right now, as an example, Brooke, what's your closing rate on showed calls?"
[12:38] "About 35%."
[12:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Closing Rate Analysis"
NÚMEROS: 35% taxa de fechamento
VISUAL: Taxa de fechamento indicando qualidade do pré-call, leads entre interesse geral e alto
[12:42] "Yeah, pretty good. So I would say most of the people coming through for you have a high probability to be by the time they reach the closers they're realistically within these two categories here somewhere between general and high interest which is great that represents that your pre-call information is doing awesome"
[13:00] "like confirmation page best practices value dense email sequences hammer them putting them through the VSSL whatever the setters andor closers might be getting to them in information that's all solid"
[13:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Real Problem"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Setters interpretando baixo interesse como desqualificação, comparação com leads de maior interesse
[13:18] "what's sounds to be the disconnect here and this happens for a lot of businesses is is it sounds like you have setters that are talking to people that are at a lower interest level in direct comparison to the two higher interest level types of leads and they might be interpreting those people as unqualified as a result of lower interest. Does that make sense in a line or no?"
[13:40] "That's exactly what I feel like the problem is. Like I feel like there's um a whole bunch of people who are on the line who could close who are just being dqed because we almost have an overabundance of calls."
[13:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Abundance Problem"
NÚMEROS: 30% cancelamento mencionado
VISUAL: Excesso de calls levando ao cancelamento dos 30% inferiores, dinheiro sendo deixado na mesa
[13:58] "So um there's just so many calls that we can book in that the bottom 30% just get canned even though there's a lot of cash there."
[14:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Abundance Behavior Example"
NÚMEROS: $50, $100, $140 gorjetas mencionadas
VISUAL: Comparação de comportamento com gorjetas: abundância leva a gastos maiores, recursos não são vistos como escassos
[14:10] "When we're in abundance, right? Like Brooke, you make a lot of money. How what what do you tip a valet?"
[14:15] "50 bucks, 100 bucks."
[14:17] "Okay. I know people that like they come to my building where they have a valet and they'll try they'll try to tip him like five bucks or 10 bucks. Like why do you and I like I just came out of this sprinter van. I I gave the guy 140 bucks. Why do you and I give people 50 100 bucks? Why do you think that is?"
[14:35] "Because we have it's it means less to us than someone"
[14:38] "excess. Yeah. It's just excess. Abundance and excess. When we're in abundance and excess, we'll naturally treat the resources that we have available to us as, you know, they're not scarce. They're not scarce by any means, which obviously changes the behavior dramatically that we're going to have and follow through with. Correct."
[14:58] "Correct. Yeah."
[15:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Comparison Bias in Setters"
NÚMEROS: 3 tipos de pessoas mencionados
VISUAL: Três categorias de leads, viés de comparação se desenvolvendo independente do volume de leads
[15:02] "Okay. So, watch this. There's a few very important things to understand here. So, number one, SDRs, okay, when they deal because think about this, guys, like they deal with every one of these ranges."
[15:18] "And let's label these types of people. Let's label them person type number one, person type number two, and person type number three. Whether they have a fuckload of leads or whether they have very few leads, human beings develop a bias."
[15:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Comparison Bias Mechanics"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Processo de desenvolvimento de viés: comparação de leads → julgamento de qualificação baseado em interesse → leads curiosos parecem desqualificados
[15:38] "Okay? And the biases that we have and that we act through in this case come down to comparison bias more than anything else. If I compare leads and I'm trying to think with who's qualified and who's unqualified and I'm taking people who are at an interest level that's already high or even an interest level that's already at general, I'm naturally going to consider those much more qualified leads"
[16:00] "but that doesn't necessarily actually mean that they're qualified or not. It's just an interest level that I'm gauging more so than anything else."
[16:10] "And then when somebody comes along who has a qualified level of interest that's only curious, they think, 'Oh, this person like they'll they'll give shorter answers.' Think about this. Somebody who's curious, are they going to give as good of answers? Are they going to sound as enthusiastic about the opportunity? Right? Are they going to come through excited?"
[16:30] "It's like, well, no. Those these traits might not be there. They might not exist, right? That doesn't mean that they're not financially qualified. That doesn't mean that they can't be sold. It might just mean they need more information in comparison to the leads that are already at a higher interest level."
[16:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Information = Action Principle"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Equação fundamental: Information → Action, gap de informação cria baixo interesse
[16:50] "Information is what gets people to act. Okay? Information leads to action. Okay? This is where most people really [__] up with their setters."
[17:02] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Critical Mistake"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Erro comum: treinar setters → desenvolver viés de comparação → leads financeiramente qualificados com baixo interesse são bloqueados
[17:05] "Okay? They'll train their setters and let the setters develop those comparison biases. And through time, financially qualified leads at lower interest levels aren't allowed through."
[17:18] "Yeah, that's% because of the comparison."
[17:22] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Cancellation Creep Over Time"
NÚMEROS: Taxa de cancelamento aumentando gradualmente
VISUAL: Mesmo funil, taxa de cancelamento subindo ao longo do tempo devido ao viés de comparação
[17:25] "Over time, this the SDR is like we've had the same funnel, but our cancellation percentage has slowly creeped up over time because of exactly that. I think"
[17:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Real Setter Job"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Função correta do setter: não DQ o lead → fornecer informação personalizada → aumentar nível de interesse → levar à ação
[17:38] "and think about this, Brooke and everybody else listen. Okay. And this is really important you understand this. In that specific scenario where the person's financially qualified, capable of purchasing, can technically be deemed a good lead, and whether there's abundance or excess or just a normal daily lead flow that they're working with."
[18:00] "If I allow comparison bias to creep into my closer psyches, I am [__] Okay? Because in that scenario, the setter's job is not to dq the lead and get them off the books. The setter's job becomes I need to get this person tailored information that increases their interest level which is going to lead to action."
[18:22] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Information Gap = Lower Interest"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Gap de informação cria níveis baixos de interesse, não falta de qualificação
[18:25] "Okay? It's an information gap that creates lower interest levels. You understand?"
[18:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Setter's Lazy Argument"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Viés de conservação de energia: setters preferem trabalhar apenas com leads de alto interesse que requerem menos trabalho
[18:35] "So what we have to do what we have to train our setters to do and and think about this this is also when you talk about comparison bias like the setters argument is going to be well I can do far less work if I'm just talking to leads that don't need information and I let those people through"
[18:52] "the people who are technically qualified but just at lower interest levels they are going to have to do some work they're going to have to help see what information is necessary for that person to yet that's going to lead to action."
[19:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Skin Cancer Example - Low Impact"
NÚMEROS: 50% maior chance para homens
VISUAL: Estatística genérica sobre câncer de pele, sem impacto emocional, não leva à ação
[19:10] "Let me give you a great example of this. Okay, if I told any one of you right now, hey man, you got a little spot on your arm there. Um, I don't want to freak you out or anything, but I think that might be skin cancer."
[19:25] "Like my mom, she had skin cancer. And her I mean before she even knew she had it, like she had something on her arm that looked just like that. And I mean, dude, what you got there is I'm not joking. I'm not trying to freak you out or anything. I mean, it's pretty [__] similar, dude. you know, I would go get a scan if I were you."
[19:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Generic Statistic - No Action"
NÚMEROS: 50% estatística mencionada
VISUAL: Comparação: estatística genérica vs. observação pessoal específica, impacto na ação
[19:48] "Versus if I just said, 'Hey guys, uh, apparently men have a 50% higher chance of getting skin cancer because next to none of us ever wear [__] sunscreen when we go outside.' So, it's like I'm rolling around top down in my cars all day, just ripping around in the Miami sunshine out here, enjoying the heck out of the sun."
[20:10] "Makes me feel great. I'm not thinking of skin cancer. I don't even give a [__] about that statistic that 50% of of of of uh the status 50% higher for men getting skin cancer than women. That doesn't lead me to action. You see, but if somebody"
[20:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Dermatologist Authority Example"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Amplificação através de autoridade: dermatologista apontando problema específico + experiência pessoal = ação imediata
[20:30] "Let me let me amplify it further. If a [] dermatologist was the one that pointed out that first scenario where a dermatologist that I just coincidentally run into while I'm at a restaurant or some [] is like sitting next to me and goes, 'Hey man, just want to point out to you I think you got something on your arm that should get checked out there.'"
[20:50] "'Not trying to freak you out, but you know, my mom, she had something very similar. And I had a few patients recent, I'm a dermatologist, had a few patients recently had had that same thing. And there was that actually ended up being a little melanoma. Um, maybe you want to go get a scan and get it checked out.'"
[21:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Information Difference = Action"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Comparação clara: mesma informação, contexto diferente, resultado completamente diferente em termos de ação
[21:12] "It's like, can you see how the differences in information there would lead to action? You understand?"
[21:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Spray and Pray Marketing"
NÚMEROS: 15-20 vídeos, 5-20 vídeos, 6 emails/dia, 30-50 short form, 15-30 long form
VISUAL: Abordagem de marketing ampla: múltiplos vídeos na página de confirmação, múltiplos emails, muito conteúdo porque não sabemos qual vai funcionar
[21:22] "So, it's like, think about this. When when as marketers we attempt to get people information, it's like a [] spray and prey. It's like when you look at all these steps up here, it's like the confirmation page has 15 to 20 videos on it. Like five to 20 videos on it because we have no [] clue which one of the videos that specific person needs that's going to increase their interest level and inspire action. So, we put as many as we can."
[21:50] "It's like it's the same thing with emails. It's like why the [__] are we sending six a day? It's like cuz I have no clue which one's going to lead to the action. I have like a general idea, but I mean that's why I have all the emails. I I have a general idea that all of them will technically do something. So, I got to send all of them. I don't know which one's going to have the impact."
[22:12] "It's the same thing with my hammer them strategy. It's like, why do I have to put 30 to 50 short form pieces of content and 15 to 30 long form pieces of content? It's like for a majority of you sitting here, I don't even know which [__] YouTube video specifically inspired you to take action."
[22:30] "I just know in general that YouTube helps, you know? I don't I don't know if it was one video or a combination of videos. I have no [__] clue. But but it's like that's why I have to keep churning out more videos. It's like it can't just be a oneanddone thing."
[22:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Setter/Closer Advantage"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Vantagem única: conversa permite coletar informação → julgar nível de interesse → enviar informação específica categorizada
[22:50] "Whereas when you look at a setter specifically, you look at a setter and a closer specifically, what they can do is based on the conversation that they're having, they can collect information about what that person's needs are and they can judge where they're at on the interest spectrum."
[23:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Organized Information Library"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Lista massiva e bem organizada no Google Doc: sales assets, trust assets, categorizados por necessidade do lead
[23:10] "And then your job as the business owner in this case is to have a monster list of well organized information that can be sent to leads categorically based on what that lead needs in order to get their interest levels up. That's where most people [__] up."
[23:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Business Model Example"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Exemplo prático: Google Doc com todos os assets sobre modelo de negócio organizados
[23:30] "So, as an example, if Brooke's getting questions about the business model, why wouldn't Brooke have a massive list inside a Google Doc of every sales asset and trust asset and piece of information he's got that relates to how the business model works? Why wouldn't he have that? You understand?"
[23:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Setter Script - Information Offering"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Script completo do setter oferecendo múltiplos formatos de informação: SOPs, FAQs escritos, vídeos, podcasts, testimonials, case studies
[23:50] "And why wouldn't the setter say, 'Hey, Brooke, actually really appreciate you asking that question about the business model. I I can do two things, man. First of all, I can explain it to you right now, but second of all, generally what people like you need in order to make this a priority and like actually take action on starting this whole thing. You just need information.'"
[24:12] "'I have a handful of things I can send to you. I have SOPs that explain how the business model works. Like I have essentially like written FAQ things that I can send to you. I have a handful of videos I can send to you. As a matter of fact, I even have like a longer podcast if you prefer it broke down that way explaining how the business model works.'"
[24:32] "'Um, I also have some testimonials and case studies that outside of the results that they're articulating, they also articulate how the business model works. Uh, any one of those sound like a good preference for you for which one you might want to bias towards'"
[24:48] "and whether the person Oh, yeah. I don't know. I don't know, Jeremy. You just send me whatever. You know, it's like, all right, no problem. And my goal from there as the setter would be to ensure I get that person the information they need."
[25:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Human Laziness Problem"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Problema fundamental: humanos são naturalmente preguiçosos, conservam energia, setters preferem leads fáceis
[25:08] "Okay. If you want to really amplify this and make the setter's job easier, because here's the thing that I hate about giving this advice, humans are naturally and inherently lazy. Okay? We always bias towards energy conservation."
[25:22] "So, it's like when I am a setter and a closer, why wouldn't I bias towards the people that are already at higher levels of interest that are more probable to close? That's what I get paid on, right?"
[25:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Solution: Incentive + Easy System"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Solução dupla: criar incentivo + tornar ação fácil de executar
[25:38] "It's like I have to either have an incentive and or an easy way to be able to take action on what I just articulated to you. So, let's make the setter's job easy. Okay?"
[25:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "CRM Disposition System"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Sistema de dropdown no CRM: seleção de razão de baixo interesse → aplicado ao registro do contato → automação contextual

[26:08] "Uh just what I'm trying to articulate is is like inside of the CRM if you have a little drop- down field that has what the reasons are that the person has a low interest level and they just all the setter has to do is select that specific box and that applies it to the contact record in the CRM."
[26:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Contextual Marketing Automation"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados

[26:30] "We can then as marketers set up contextual marketing automation that sends tailored communication to that individual about what they specifically needed to get their interest levels higher."
[26:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Joint Strategy: Manual + Automated"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Estratégia conjunta: outreach manual do setter/closer + outreach automatizado contextual = probabilidade dramaticamente aumentada
[26:48] "And we would want to do that in conjunction ideally with the setters or the closers just manually sending some stuff prior to the call. Okay, this joint strategy of manual outreach in addition to the automated contextual outreach makes it so one or the other happens either way, which dramatically increases the probability that we get that interest level up prior to the call."
[27:12] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Low Interest = Education Call"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Problema do closer: baixo interesse = call educacional não call de vendas, reclamações sobre qualidade de lead
[27:15] "Okay? Because that's really all that matters. When closers get on calls with people that have low interest levels, they're going to say that they're too early in the sales process. They're going to [__] about lead quality. And they're not wrong technically because they're not talking to people who are there to have a sales conversation. They're talking to people who are there to have an educationbased call."
[27:38] "So the setters, their job in their eyes is I'm trying to tee people up onto the calendars that are actually probable to close."
[27:48] "Brooke, talk about your incentives real quick. How do you pay your setters?"
[27:52] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Current Setter Compensation"

[27:55] "So, the ones who do the dialing for the calls just get um I believe 2 or 3k a month base and then they get paid commission based on um other things like like our DM setter gets a commission if a deal closes. If they call a webinar buyer and the webinar buyer closes, they get a commission, but they're not actually getting paid commission off of these because it's every single one is called within a minute. So, that's more the base pay."
[28:22] "Yeah. And so, think about that, right? It's like there's not even an incentive that you have to pass through calls. It's just a part of their base pay. It's just like a function of their job."
[28:32] "The calling the booked calls for the VSSL. Yes."
[28:35] "Okay. Now, watch this. Right. What's an ideal time frame that a lead would close for you, Brooke?"

[30:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Mathematical Reality"

[31:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Cultural Leadership Concept"

[31:40] "Okay, a good example of this u you know here in America a lot of people like to complain about the polarization that we experience and the polarization comes from the lack of cultural leadership. Cultural leadership is really just somebody preaching all day about the beliefs we all collectively hold."
[32:00] "And as a result of nobody constantly preaching collective beliefs that we all hold true to ourselves collectively, we individually start to define our beliefs instead."
[32:12] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Beliefs Control Behavior"

[34:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Not Financial Qualification"

[34:42] "So again, it's it's an info gap. You understand? That's what has to be resolved. That's how we attack it. That's how we justify leads having higher prioritization on what we need to get them right."
[34:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Good Info Requirement"

[34:58] "It has to remember it has to be good info. Like the skin cancer thing I told you, I can't just tell y'all a statistic like, 'Hey man, men have 50% greater chances of skin cancer than women and h and expect y'all to go get [__] skin cancer tests.'"
[35:15] "You know, think about it, guys. It's like if I literally am a doctor and I approach you and I like look at you intently for a few seconds and then I tell you that same information, dude, what's the difference? It's substantial. One leads to action and one doesn't. You get what I'm saying?"

[35:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Failure Pattern"

[35:50] "Setters develop their own inherent beliefs. Set up setters develop biases from qualified higher interest leads comparatively to lower interest leads. [38:12] "It's really easy to abandon people. You know, systems and automation and computers like, dude, that's always going to be easy and logical and straightforward and it's not going to have emotion and it's not going to have biases and it's just going to just going to do what we need it to do."

[44:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "AI As Addition Not Replacement"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Benefício principal: dar respostas ao prospect + enviar trust assets, AI trabalha lado a lado com humanos
[44:02] "But the main benefit of it is just to get the prospect those answers to those questions or even send out those trust assets. The AI can also do that as well. So just thinking of it like that and not a human replacement, more of like addition to your setters to have right now um to kind of automate that follow-up process and work hand in hand with the uh the humans."
[44:28] "Yeah, it makes sense. All right, very good. Anybody else have any questions or anything you want to mute yourself and ask away? Anything we need to address to help you guys turn this into action going once."
[44:42] "Brooke, any final thoughts on this you want to share with the people since you're dealing with this problem? Anything you want to add to it?"
[44:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "AI Transcript Analysis Idea"
NÚMEROS: 6 setters mencionados
VISUAL: Ideia: exportar transcrições → AI analisa → automação contextual baseada em transcrição vs. abordagem em massa
[44:52] "Uh, no. I think I think yeah, I have a ton a ton of stuff to put in. The only other thing I was thinking, I'm not sure if this is what your tool does, Chris, or if it would do this at all, is um I wonder if there's a way to like export the transcripts from these calls and then based upon the transcript have AI analyze it and then do contextual automation that way"
[45:15] "instead of like the mass approach like still have the mass assets we make now, but um sending them specific things based upon the call automatically I just think would be at scale simpler than than a team of six setters doing however many calls a day."
[45:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "N8N Integration Possibility"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Possibilidade técnica: N8N + análise de call + CRM + comunicações contextuais automatizadas
[45:35] "Very good question. I would imagine that there is through likely something with NADM and tying that into call analysis and your CRM and then doing what I articulated to fire off those contextual communications."
[45:50] "I'm not sure if as an example like Chris's technology with simple AI has that ability to do so too with tying into to N8 to do something similar but yeah I mean that'd be that'd be pretty sick a lot of sense and I feel like we're there with technology be more than capable of doing that so if you end up trying something like that that'd be sick to hear about it."
[46:12] "Yep. Of course."
[46:15] "All right cool. Anybody else got anything?"
[46:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Q3 Mastermind Announcement"
NÚMEROS: Julho 12-13, Q3
VISUAL: Anúncio de mastermind: data, expectativa de grande participação ao contrário do usual Q3 mais leve
[46:20] "Very good. Shorter call today. Appreciate you guys being here. upcoming Q3 mastermind July 12th and 13th. We got a fuckload of people coming. Normally, I would set the expectation Q3 masterminds are generally a little lighter. Uh we are not shaping up for that at all. This seems to be a very normal mastermind in terms of attendance comparatively."
[46:42] "So, looking forward to seeing everybody who's going to make it out. You have any questions about anything, let me know. Um all the details for the mastermind are pinned at the top of the group chat. Uh be sure to take a look at that and then let me know after."
[46:58] "All right, cool. Talk soon, everybody."
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
30% taxa de cancelamento de calls
35% taxa de fechamento em calls que aparecem
Custo por call quase dobrou após qualificações mais rígidas
$2-3K base mensal para setters
$50-100 bônus proposto por fechamento rápido
2-3 dias timeframe ideal de fechamento
4-5 dias timeframe usual de fechamento
50% redução de bônus após 7 dias
20-25 minutos de VSSL forçado
15-20 vídeos na página de confirmação
6 emails por dia enviados
30-50 peças de conteúdo short form
15-30 peças de conteúdo long form
6 setters na equipe
Q3 Mastermind: Julho 12-13
Multi-8 figures e million-dollar months mencionados como benchmark
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Problema Identificado:
Setters cancelando 30% das calls por razões que deveriam ser tratadas na sales call
Leads preocupados com timing ou querendo follow-up em um mês sendo desqualificados prematuramente
Custo por call dobrou após apertar qualificações
Call Funnel Structure:
Ad → VSSL Page → Application → Booking
Confirmation Page Best Practices
Value Dense Email Sequences
Setters/Closers Manual Outreach
Hammer Them Campaign
Escala de Interesse (Interest Scale):
Curiosity (mais baixo)
General Interest
High Interest
Conviction
SOLD
Money Zone: entre Conviction e Sold
Problema do Comparison Bias:
Setters desenvolvem viés comparando leads de baixo vs. alto interesse
Leads financeiramente qualificados mas com baixo interesse são cancelados
Viés aumenta com tempo independente de abundância ou escassez de leads
Solução Proposta - Três Pilares:
A. Informação Personalizada:
Criar biblioteca massiva organizada no Google Doc
Categorizar por tipo de objeção/necessidade
Incluir: SOPs, FAQs escritos, vídeos, podcasts, testimonials, case studies
Setter oferece múltiplos formatos e deixa lead escolher
B. Sistema CRM com Dispositions:
Dropdown field no CRM para razão de baixo interesse
Seleção aplica ao registro do contato
Dispara automação contextual de marketing
Envia comunicação específica sobre necessidade identificada
C. Estratégia Conjunta Manual + Automação:
Setter envia informação manualmente antes da call
Sistema automatizado também envia baseado em seleção CRM
Garante que uma ou outra acontece
Aumenta dramaticamente probabilidade de elevar interesse
Nova Estrutura de Incentivos:
Base pay mantido ($2-3K/mês)
Bônus de $50-100 por fechamento em 2-3 dias
50% do bônus por fechamento em 7 dias
Potencial de milhares extras por mês
Incentiva priorização de leads de baixo interesse para enviar informação
Princípio Information = Action:
Gap de informação cria baixo interesse
Informação certa leva à ação
Exemplo: estatística genérica vs. observação específica de autoridade
Contexto e autoridade amplificam impacto da informação
Abordagem Spray and Pray do Marketing:
Múltiplos vídeos porque não sabemos qual funciona
Múltiplos emails porque não sabemos qual converte
Muito conteúdo porque não sabemos qual inspira ação
Setters têm vantagem: podem identificar necessidade específica
AI Integration Possibilities:
Simple AI para speed to lead
Responder 1-3 perguntas críticas rapidamente
Enviar trust assets automaticamente
AI como adição, não substituição de humanos
Fail-safe system: humanos primeiro → AI follow-up
Exportar transcrições → AI analisa → automação contextual via N8N
Análise de Calls com LLM:
Usar Gong ou exportar transcrições
Importar em ChatGPT/Claude
Treinar LLM para avaliar (escala 0-100)
Identificar se perguntas soam egoístas ou amigáveis
Automação possível com N8N
ESTRUTURA:
Função Real do Setter:
NÃO apenas qualificar e agendar
NÃO apenas tirar leads ruins do calendário
SIM: Coletar dados para CRM
SIM: Identificar gap de informação
SIM: Enviar informação personalizada que leva à ação
SIM: Aumentar probabilidade de show-up
SIM: Elevar nível de interesse do lead
Primeira Interação é Crítica:
Analogia do restaurante Michelin
Experiência de reserva faz parte da experiência total
Internet marketing negligencia isso
Intenção egoísta transparece para o lead
Começa experiência de forma violenta
Liderança Cultural Necessária:
Crenças controlam comportamento
Vendedores precisam lições reiteradas constantemente
Falta de liderança cultural leva à polarização
Indivíduos definem próprias crenças sem liderança
Líder carismático prega crenças coletivas constantemente
Responsabilidade do Líder:
Não é chato ou difícil
Algumas reuniões por semana
Pregar do topo sobre o que quer que pensem
Projetar crenças que beneficiam organização E ajudam clientes
Pode ser delegado a sales/setter manager
Missão Além do Dinheiro:
Sim, queremos escalar e ficar ricos
MAS também impactamos vidas substancialmente
Cada lead não ajudado = pessoa deixada na poeira
Qualidade de vida ruim mantida por decisão do setter
Responsabilidade individual de cada setter
Teste Antes de Mudanças Grandes:
Sempre repetir ações bem-sucedidas
O que te trouxe aqui funcionou
Mudanças grandes devem ser testadas
Não ir all-in sem validação
Pode ser ambos: pessoas E automação
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Base pay setters: $2-3K/mês
Bônus proposto: $50-100 por deal rápido
Redução bônus: 50% após 7 dias
Potencial extra mensal: milhares de dólares
Closing rate atual: 35% em showed calls
Cancellation rate: 30% das calls bookadas
Custo por call: quase dobrou após qualificações apertadas
Benchmark revenue: multi-8 figures anuais, million-dollar months
ROI do tempo/dinheiro em geração de leads sendo desperdiçado com one-and-done approach
DURAÇÃO: ~47 minutos FORMATO: Group coaching call, screen sharing com slides visuais, discussão interativa focada em resolver problema específico de Brooke com SDR cancellation rates e comparison bias
